title: Understanding RCD and MCB trip types
subtitle: What trips each device, how to reset them, and what trips mean for the circuit
excerpt: An explanation of the difference between an MCB (overcurrent protection) and an RCD (earth fault protection): what causes each to trip, how to diagnose the fault, and what action to take before resetting.
sourceNotes: IET Wiring Regulations guidance (BS 7671, 18th Edition). Napit and NICEIC consumer guidance (publicly available). Collins Complete DIY Manual.

content.text: A consumer unit contains at least two types of protective device. An
protects against overcurrent (too many appliances or a short circuit). An
protects against earth faults (current flowing through a person or damaged insulation to earth). They trip for different reasons and require different diagnostic steps.

An MCB trips when current exceeds its rated amperage. Common causes are overload (too many high-wattage appliances on one circuit) or a short circuit (live touching neutral or earth, usually in a damaged cable or appliance). A tripped MCB sits in a middle position between on and off.

Unplug all appliances on the circuit. Push the MCB fully to OFF, then to ON. If it trips again immediately, there is a short circuit in the wiring or a fixed appliance: do not keep resetting. If it holds, plug appliances in one at a time to identify the faulty one.

An RCD trips when it detects imbalance between live and neutral current, indicating leakage to earth. 30 mA is the standard trip threshold for domestic socket circuits. Common causes are a damaged appliance, wet conditions, or a fault in the fixed wiring.

Unplug everything on circuits protected by the RCD. Reset the RCD. If it holds, plug appliances back one at a time to find the one causing the trip. If the RCD trips immediately on reset with nothing plugged in, the fault is in the fixed wiring: the circuit needs testing by an electrician.
In a modern consumer unit with

fitted on individual circuits, each circuit has its own earth-fault and overcurrent protection combined in one device. A trip affects only that circuit, not a group of circuits sharing an RCD. This makes fault diagnosis easier: the tripped RCBO identifies the circuit directly.

Every RCD in the consumer unit has a test button (usually marked T or TEST). Press it once a month with the power on: the RCD should trip immediately. Reset it after the test. If the RCD does not trip when the test button is pressed, it has failed mechanically and must be replaced. A failed RCD provides no protection even though it looks normal.
